content.full_text:
SUPERSEDED
RAIMEHTA
IN PARI
FAIR
POLITICAL PRACTICES
COMMISSION
(atcsO//)
February 16,
1996
Jonathan Sharkey
Counc ± imember
City of Port Hueneme
250 North Ventura Road
Port Hueneme, California
93041
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—96—029
Dear Mr.
Sharkey:
This is in response to your request for advice regarding your
responsibilities as a public official under the conflict—of--
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
QUESTION
May you apply for
a Residential Rehabilitation Loan
administered by the City of Port Hueneme and funded by Community
Development Block Grants
(“CDBG”)
and funds from the County of
Ventura?
CONCLUSION
You may apply for a
CDBG loan so long as it is awarded
pursuant to objective criteria unrelated to your status as a
public official.
You may not participate in your official
capacity in any decision which may modify your liability under the
loan in the amount of $250 or more over a twelve month period.
Our advice is limited to the provisions of the Act.
Other
provisions of the law,
such as Government Code Section 1090,
may
have some bearing on your situation.
The Commission does not have
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18995.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—96—029
Page
2
jurisdiction to interpret or enforce Section 1090 since the
Commission’s jurisdiction is limited to the Political Reform Act.
Questions concerning Section 1090 should be addressed to the
Attorney General’s Office or your County Counsel.
FACTS
Since 1978, the City of Port Hueneme has operated a
Residential Loan Program in target neighborhoods of the city.
Early on, that program was funded by Small Cities Community
Development Block Grants.
Since 1986,
however, the city has been
a subrecipient of
CDBG funds through the Urban County Entitlement
administered by the County of Ventura.
Today,
your rehabilitation
program uses a combination of local funds and
CDBG funds; the
latter resource being reserved solely for noncreditworthy lower—
income owner—occupants.
Late last year,
you were one of three new city councilmembers
elected to the council.
Your residence is within the
rehabilitation program’s top priority neighborhood and is among
the oldest housing stock in the city.
The age, condition and
location of your home is consistent with the group of dwellings
the city has considered its highest priority target and your
income is such that you may meet the economic requirements for
receiving
CDBG funds.
Participation in the program would allow
you to receive a benefit which is generally available to other
people with similar financial qualifications and adequate equity
residing in the same neighborhood.
You propose to announce your intention to participate in the
rehabilitation program as a topic of discussion on the agenda at a
regular public meeting of the city council.
The discussion would
include a disclosure of the nature of the potential conflict and
address the concerns of the other members of the council and the
general public.
ANALYSIS
Economic Interests
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
As a city council member of the City of Port Hueneme,
you are
a “public official” as defined in the Act.
(Section 82048.)
Thus,
you may not make, participate in making or use your official
position to influence a governmental decision in which you know or
have reason to know you have a financial interest.
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have


File No.
A—96—029
Page
3
a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally,
on the official,
a member of his or her
immediate family,
or on any of the following:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth one
thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand
dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institution in
the regular course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status, aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to, received by or promised to the public
official within
12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
(e)
Any donor of,
or any intermediary or agent for
a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating two hundred
fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by,
or promised to the public official within
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
The amount of the value of gifts specified by this
subdivision shall be adjusted biennially by the
Commission to equal the same amount determined by the
Commission pursuant to subdivision
(d)
of Section 89504.
You must disqualify yourself from any decision which has .a
material financial effect on your economic interests.
You have an
economic interest in your real property because your home is
presumably worth more than $1,000.
(Section 87103(b).)
In addition,
in some instances,
a public official may have an
economic interest in the source of a loan because the loan will be
considered income to the official.
As explained below,
however,
you will not have an economic interest in the city by virtue of
receiving CDBG funds because CDBG funds will not be considered
income under the Act.
Section 82030(a)
generally defines income to include loans.
However,
Section 82030(b)
states in relevant part:


File No.
A—96—029
Page
4
(b)
“Income”
...
does not include:
*
*
*
(8)
Any loan or loans from a commercial
lending institution which are made in the lender’s
regular course of business on terms available to
members of the public without regard to official
status if:
(A)
Used to purchase, refinance the purchase
of,
or for improvements to,
the principal residence
of filer;
or
(B)
The balance owed does not exceed ten
thousand dollars
($10,000).
*
*
*
Section 82030(b) (8)
In the Alperin Advice Letter,
1-94-340,
we determined that a
government agency may function as a commercial lending institution
when the agency provides loans to persons without regard to the
persons’ official status.
The receipt of such a loan does not
create any greater potential for bias than loans from a commercial
lending institution made in the regular course of business.
In
this instance, the city administers redevelopment funds received.
from Community Development Block Grants and an Urban County
Entitlement administered by the County of Ventura.
The loan
program targets specific neighborhoods and administers
redevelopment loans to low income owner—occupants.
Recipients of
the loans are chosen based on their location within the
preestablished redevelopment area,
financial qualifications and
the amount of equity in their home.
The program is administered
by the city pursuant to these pre-established loan criteria.
We conclude that for the purposes of Section 82030(b) (8) the
term “commercial lending institution” includes the loan of
CDBG
funds when the funds are provided to persons pursuant to objective
criteria without regard to the persons’ official status.
2
Consequently, the
CDBG loan funds you are considering applying for
will not be considered income if they are awarded to you without
regard to your official status.
Because the
CDBG funds you may receive from the city are not
considered income, the city will not become a source of income to
you for disqualification purposes.
Regulation 18702.1,
however,
states that an official must be disqualified from any decision
2
To the extent this advice conflicts with the Tripp Advice
Letter,
No.
A-92-056, the Tripp letter is superseded.


File No.
A—96—029
Page
5
that will “result in the personal expenses,
income, assets,
or
liabilities” of the official or his immediate family increasing or
decreasing by at least $250 in any 12-month period,
excluding your
interest in real property.
Accordingly,
you may not participate
in any decision by the city which will result in an increase or
decrease in your expenses or liabilities in relation to the
CDBG
loan funds by at least
$250 in any 12-month period.
In addition,
you will need to consider the effect of any
decision on your real property interest.
You have not mentioned
what types of decisions may come up that will affect your real
property.
Pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, if your interest in
real property is directly involved in the decision,. for instance
if the redevelopment area or the redevelopment plan is amended,
you must disqualify yourself from any participation in the
decision.
Please consult Regulation 18702.1(3)
to determine
whether your property will be directly involved in any decision
before the council.
Further, if your real property is not
directly involved,
as defined in Regulation 18702.1,
you must
consult Regulation 18702.3 to determine if your real property
interest is indirectly involved in the decision and the decision
will have a material financial effect on your property interest.
If the decision will have a material financial effect on your real
property interest,
you may not participate.
Participation in Governmental Decisions
Participation in governmental decisions has been interpreted
broadly in furtherance of the goals of the Act.
Thus,
where a
governmental decision will have a reasonably foreseeable, material
financial effect on an official’s economic interest, the official
must not vote on the decision or make contacts with,
appear
before,
or otherwise attempt to influence,
any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the official’s agency concerning the
governmental decision.
(Regulations 18700 and 18700.1.)
This
prohibition also applies to any agency appointed by or subject to
the budgetary control of the official’s agency.
(Regulation
18700.1(a).)
Where a conflict exists, there is an exception to this rule
where the public official is participating in the same manner as
any other private citizen.
Both Regulation 18700(d) (2)
and
Regulation 18700.1(b) (1)
allow a public official to appear,
as any
member of the general public, before an agency in the course of
its prescribed governmental function, to represent himself or
herself on matters related solely to his or her personal
interests.
A “personal interest” includes an interest in real
property which is wholly owned by the official or members of his
or her immediate family.
(Regulation 18700.1(b) (1).)
Thus, the Act allows a public official to comment,
as a
member of the public, at public meetings,
forums,
or hearings on
issues related solely to the effect of a decision on his or her
personal interests.
Once the official has formally disqualified


File No.
A—96—029
Page
6
himself or herself by announcing his or her disqualification and
the reason therefor on the public record (Regulation 18700(b) (5),
his or her comments should be made under the same procedures
applicable to comments from members of the public.
Your participation in any discussion of
CDBG loan issues,
or
modifications of the redevelopment program, therefore, must be
limited to your personal interests,
and you should take care to
clarify that you are not acting in any official capacity and are
not representing any other person’s or group of person’s
interests.
(Larsen Advice Letter,
No.
A-87—151.)
In sum,
you may apply for the loan so long as you apply for
the loan as a private citizen and receive the funds based on the
objective pre-established criteria, without regard to your
official status.
You will be disqualified from participation in
any decision which will increase or decrease your liabilities
under the loan of $250 or more within a twelve month period or
which will have a material financial effect on your real property
interest pursuant to Regulations 18702.1(a) (3)
and 18702.3,
copies
enclosed.
Consequently,
you may participate in decisions
regarding the redevelopment program only if you are limited to
representing your personal interests.
As noted above,
you should
consult the Attorney General’s Office or your County Counsel for
questions concerning Government Code Section 1090.
If you have any further questions, please feel free to
contact me at
(916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
/
/
/ /
By
Liaje Randolph
Counsel,
Legal Division
SGC:LR:ak
